case_number: Cr.A No. 27-D of 2024 with Cr.M No 339-D/2024
legal_issues: jurisdiction of Consumer Protection Court in alleged medical negligence cases
non-application of judicial mind by the trial court
case: Cr.A No. 27-D of 2024 Dr. Hafiz Muhammad Tahir Vs Muhammad Salam
remarks:
This Court has already recognized and affirmed the jurisdiction of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission in matters relating to medical negligence. In Lady Doctor Nargis v. The State in the following terms:
21. Hence, the law is clear:
(i) no FIR can be registered against a healthcare service provider or a healthcare institution on the allegations of malpractice or negligence except at the instance of the commission;
(ii) any person aggrieved of any malpractice or negligence by a healthcare service provider or institution may file a complaint u/s 13 of ‘the Act of 2015’ before the KP Healthcare Commission;
(iii) it is the duty and responsibility of the commission to expeditiously and diligently pursue and take the complaint to its legal and logical conclusion, which may also, after due process, include lodging of FIR under the relevant provisions of PPC (including section 322);
(iv) it therefore follows that any such FIR registered directly without first approaching the Healthcare Commission would be without lawful authority and liable to be quashed.
Although the said case pertained to initiation of criminal proceedings, this Court clearly held that in matters involving medical malpractice or negligence, the aggrieved person must first approach the HCC by filing a complaint under section 13 of the HCC Act. In Dr. Samdana Wahab v. The State, this Court held that allegations of medical negligence require expert and technical scrutiny and cannot be directly subjected to ordinary criminal process or inquiry by a Judicial Magistrate on the direction of an Ex-Officio Justice of Peace. The HCC is the specialized statutory forum for such matters, and the complainant must first approach it. Consequently, the order directing magisterial inquiry was set aside, while the HCC was directed to entertain the complaint afresh on merits despite limitation. This Court, in Professor Dr. NiamatUllah v. Izaz Khan (PLD 2026 Peshawar 49), has categorically held that the Consumer Protection Court has no jurisdiction to determine matters of medical negligence or malpractice, as such questions require expert assessment by the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission under section 13 of the HCC Act.
The relevant portion is reproduced below:
7. Turning to another aspect of the case whether the Consumer Protection Court is the appropriate forum to determine whether medical malpractice or negligence has occurred, it is well accepted that for a claim for medical/clinical negligence to be established, a medical practitioner has to be found to have breached a duty of care to a patient, who in turn suffers injury as a result of that breach. Demonstrating that a doctor has breached the duty of care is the first major hurdle in any negligence case but this is not always clear cut. There is certainly scope for genuine differences of opinion when it comes to diagnosis and treatment. The complainant\'s contention is that the Consumer Protection Court lacks the jurisdiction and it is the Commission that is to make an assessment in that regard in view of the standards envisaged under section 13 of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission Act,2015. Needless to say, liability claims for defective services, as envisaged under Section 13 of the Act ibid, would similarly entail a breach of a duty to be determined by the commission under section 13, it reads:
13. Complaints.---(1) An aggrieved person may, within sixty days from the date of knowledge of the cause of action, file a complaint against a healthcare service provider or healthcare establishment by submitting an application in writing supported by an affidavit, national identity card number and address of the aggrieved person. (2) The Commission shall not entertain an anonymous or pseudonymous complaint against a private health care service provider or healthcare establishment. (3) The Commission shall investigate in a transparent manner, the complaints relating to quality of health services, health services or system and medical negligence. (4) The Commission shall prescribe the procedure for the conduct of investigation to be carried out by the Commission under this Act.
Reliance in this respect is placed on PLD 2019 Lahore 429 Dr. Riaz Qadeer Khan vs Presiding Officer, District Consumer Court, Sargodha and others, the relevant is:
For what has been discussed above, we are persuaded to hold that Healthcare Commission is the only competent forum to investigate into the allegations of maladministration or malpractice by a health service provider and the Consumer Court has no jurisdiction to adjudicate upon such matters. Resultantly, all these writ petitions are allowed and the complaints pending before the learned Consumer Court are directed to be returned for its presentation before the appropriate forum.
8. In view of the above provision of law, the respondent No.l/complainant has not filed any complaint rather approached the Consumer Protection Court, which has no jurisdiction to try such like complaints.
The ratio of the aforesaid judgment directly governs the present controversy; consequently, the complaint ought to have been presented before the HCC, being the competent statutory forum.The Honorable Lahore High Court, in Dr. Riaz Qadeer Khan v. Presiding Officer, District Consumer Court, Sargodha (PLD 2019 Lahore 429), held that the Healthcare Commission is the only competent forum to investigate allegations of maladministration or malpractice by a healthcare service provider, and that the Consumer Court has no jurisdiction to adjudicate such matters. In Dr. Saiqa Yousaf v. The State (2024 P Cr. LJ 1852 Islamabad), Honorable Islamabad High Court held that where a special statutory mechanism exists to deal with medical negligence, such as the Healthcare Commission or PMDC, the same must be invoked first, and no criminal proceedings can be initiated without exhausting that remedy. The Court further held that determination of medical negligence requires expert opinion, and in the absence of such technical evidence, registration of FIR or continuation of criminal proceedings is without lawful justification.Thus, in light of the above-referred judgments, and keeping in view the overriding effect conferred upon the HCC Act under section 33, the answer to question No. 1 is that the Consumer Protection Court has no jurisdiction to entertain or decide complaints relating to alleged medical negligence in the presence of the Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Health Care Commission. The Khyber Pakhtunkhwa Healthcare Commission (Complaints Management) Regulations, 2022 further obligate the Commission to entertain and inquire into complaints relating to malpractice, maladministration, quackery, unethical conduct, failure to follow medical standards, documentary irregularities, and violations affecting patient rights. The Regulations specifically define “malpractice” as improper, illegal, unethical, or unskilled professional conduct and empower the Commission to initiate inquiries, conduct inspections, seek expert opinions, and pass speaking orders in accordance with principles of natural justice. Therefore, failure to disclose a valid PMDC registration number, misrepresentation of qualifications, permitting unauthorized persons to practice medicine, or allowing healthcare establishments to function in violation of prescribed standards are all matters squarely falling within the jurisdiction and regulatory competence of the HCC.
